skill.name: design-system-starter
skill.description: Guides teams through creating or improving design systems with design tokens, React component patterns, documentation templates, and accessibility checklists.
skill.summary: Create accessible design systems with reusable tokens, component patterns, and documentation guidance.
skill.author: AI Agent Hub

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but context review found documentation examples, Markdown code fences, relative imports, design token color values, and public reference URLs. No malicious intent, credential access, command execution, data exfiltration, or prompt injection evidence was found. The only notable behavior is a benign example that stores a theme preference in localStorage.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown fenced code blocks, inline file references, and TypeScript examples. No shell execution primitive, subprocess call, or command construction was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged areas are documentation syntax and component examples, not executable shell calls. Targeted searches found no child_process, exec, spawn, eval, fetch, or similar execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Relative Imports Flagged as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings are static React import examples using a relative utility path. They do not read user-controlled paths, write files, or traverse the host filesystem at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are import statements for a local class-name helper. There is no variable path input or file system API usage in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Benign Browser Storage Example
low_findings.description: The browser storage finding stores and reads only a light or dark theme preference. No credential, token, personal data, or network transfer is associated with this example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The localStorage key is theme-specific and values are limited to display preferences. The same example only updates document theme attributes and does not transmit stored data.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Public Documentation URLs Flagged as Network Risk
low_findings.description: The network findings are a design token schema URL and a link to a public contrast checker. They are references in data or documentation, not runtime calls that send data externally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are static URLs with no request code around them. No evidence was found of fetch, axios, XMLHttpRequest, or command-line network tooling.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Color Tokens and Keyboard Examples Flagged as Sensitive or Malicious
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography, C2 keyword, and key-file findings map to design token names, hex colors, size labels, and keyboard event examples. No cryptographic implementation, certificate material, malware command channel, or secret file content was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are ordinary UI vocabulary and design values. Reviewed context contains no cryptographic APIs, encoded payloads, certificate blocks, or command-and-control behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams often create inconsistent UI because tokens, components, and accessibility rules are not defined together. This skill provides a practical starter framework for reusable design systems across product teams.
actual_capabilities: Defines primitive and semantic design token structures for colors, typography, spacing, radii, and shadows.
Provides React component examples for buttons, forms, cards, modals, navigation, and theme toggles.
Explains component architecture using atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, and pages.
Includes accessibility guidance for contrast, keyboard navigation, ARIA attributes, and screen reader support.
Supplies a design system audit checklist covering components, documentation, tooling, theming, and governance.
Offers reusable templates for component implementation and W3C-style design token files.
limitations: It provides guidance and templates, not a complete installable component package.
Examples are React-oriented and may need adaptation for Vue, Angular, or native platforms.
It does not automatically validate accessibility or visual regression results.
Brand strategy, visual identity, and Figma files must be supplied by the user or team.

use_cases.title: Start a Product Design System
use_cases.description: Create the first token hierarchy, component inventory, and documentation plan for a new product UI.

use_cases.title: Standardize Frontend Components
use_cases.description: Use the React templates and examples to align component APIs, variants, accessibility, and styling conventions.

use_cases.title: Audit an Existing UI Library
use_cases.description: Apply the checklist to identify missing tokens, weak documentation, accessibility gaps, and governance issues.

prompt_templates.title: Create Token Basics
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new interface language.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define a starter design token structure for a web app. Include colors, typography, spacing, radii, and shadows.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Component Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Organize a component library before implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the design system starter to propose atoms, molecules, organisms, and page templates for my product dashboard.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Accessibility Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit these planned components for WCAG 2.1 AA concerns. Focus on contrast, keyboard navigation, labels, and ARIA usage.

prompt_templates.title: Modernize an Existing System
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced refactor of a mature UI library.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate my current component library and produce a migration plan for tokens, component APIs, theming, documentation, and governance.

output_examples.input: I need a design system foundation for a SaaS dashboard.
output: A token plan covering neutral, brand, feedback, spacing, typography, radius, and elevation decisions.
A component inventory that starts with buttons, inputs, labels, cards, modals, tables, and navigation.
A documentation outline with usage, variants, props, accessibility notes, and examples for each component.

output_examples.input: Review our component checklist before launch.
output: A prioritized audit across tokens, component states, accessibility, documentation, testing, theming, and release governance.
A list of launch blockers, follow-up improvements, and ownership areas for design and engineering teams.

output_examples.input: Help me design a theme switcher pattern.
output: A theme approach using semantic tokens, user preference persistence, system preference detection, and accessible control labels.
Guidance for testing light and dark modes across components, focus states, and contrast requirements.

best_practices: Start with semantic tokens so brand changes do not require component rewrites.
Document accessibility behavior with every component, including keyboard support and screen reader expectations.
Version changes clearly and provide migration notes before teams adopt breaking updates.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode brand colors directly inside every component.
Do not ship components without disabled, loading, focus, error, and empty states.
Do not treat documentation as optional after implementation is complete.

faq.question: Does this skill create a full component library?
faq.answer: No. It provides templates, examples, checklists, and guidance that help you build or improve one.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it support best?
faq.answer: The examples are strongest for React and TypeScript, but the design system concepts apply to other frontend stacks.

faq.question: Can it help with design tokens?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes token structures for primitives, semantic roles, typography, spacing, radii, shadows, and theming.

faq.question: Does it check accessibility automatically?
faq.answer: No. It gives WCAG-oriented guidance and checklist items, but automated and manual testing remain necessary.

faq.question: Can designers use this without writing code?
faq.answer: Yes. Designers can use the architecture, token, documentation, and audit guidance without implementing components directly.

faq.question: Why did the audit mark this as low risk?
faq.answer: Static findings were reviewed as false positives, but the report keeps a low-risk label because community content needs cautious review.
